coredata.dc:title: Functional nanomaterials as photosensitizers or delivery systems for antibacterial photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Ruijiao
Zhan, Meijun
Xu, Jingchen
Peng, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection is a global health problem that closely related to various diseases threatening human life. Although antibiotic therapy has been the mainstream treatment method for various bacterial infectious diseases for decades, the increasing emergence of bacterial drug resistance has brought enormous challenges to the application of antibiotics. Therefore, developing novel antibacterial strategies is of great importance. By producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) with photosensitizers (PSs) under light irradiation, antibacterial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) has emerged as a non-invasive and promising approach for treating bacterial infections without causing drug resistance. However, the insufficient therapeutic penetration, poor hydrophilicity, and poor biocompatibility of traditional PSs greatly limit the efficacy of aPDT. Recently, studies have found that nanomaterials with characteristics of favorable photocatalytic activity, surface plasmonic resonance, easy modification, and high drug loading capacity can improve the therapeutic efficacy of aPDT. In this review, we aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of the mechanism of nanomaterials-mediated aPDT and summarize the representative nanomaterials in aPDT, either as PSs or carriers for PSs. In addition, the combination of advanced nanomaterials-mediated aPDT with other therapies, including targeted therapy, gas therapy, and multidrug resistance (MDR) therapy, is reviewed. Also, the concerns and possible solutions of nanomaterials-based aPDT are discussed. Overall, this review may provide theoretical basis and inspiration for the development of nanomaterials-based aPDT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of nanomaterials-based PDT in antibacterial treatment.

The procedures of ROS generation in PDT. After light irradiation, the ground state PSs (S0) are activated to the excited singlet state (S1) and the excited triplet state PSs (T1) in sequence, then producing ROS in oxygen-independent/oxygen-dependent pathway (pathway 1/pathway 2), and finally returning to the initial state in circle.

A. Illustration of the synthesis of nanodumbbells ACA and its anti-biofilm effect under NIR-II light induced PDT. B. ROS detection. (i) Transient absorption kinetics probed at 3900 nm for AuNRs, Se4 with core-shell structures and Se4 with nanodumbbells structures after excitation; (ii) Decrease of the absorption of RNO in different suspensions under 1064 nm laser (1 W/cm2) irradiation for 30 min. C. In vitro antibacterial efficiency of ACA by PDT under NIR-II laser irradiation. (i) Confocal microscope images of stained bacteria and (ii) SEM images of S. aureus biofilms incubated with different materials at a concentration of 50 μg/mL under 1064 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 5 min (scale bar = 20 μM); (iii) Quantification of the biofilms. D. In vivo antibacterial efficiency of ACA by PDT under NIR-II laser irradiation. (i) Antibiofilm effect of different materials in mice; (ii) After NIR-II laser (1 W/cm2) irradiation for 5 min (except Se4-dark), photographs of mice with round soft silicone plate covered S. aureus biofilms implanted to the right leg in day 7.

A. Scheme of the TiO2/TiO2−x metasurface with potent NIR-responsive antibacterial activity on a Ti alloy implant by the aaBH method. B. The in vitro antibacterial results. (i) MTT assay and antibacterial rate of E. coli and S. aureus cultured on various specimens for 24 h and then exposed to NIR light (808 nm, 0.5 W cm−2) irradiation for 10 min; (ii) Representative images of (i). C. The in vivo antibacterial results in the animal model with one injection of bacteria. (i) Illustration of experimental design; (ii) Representative images of the S. aureus colonies; (iii) Antibacterial ratio of the different samples.

A. Schematic illustration of the preparation of GQDs-COS and its application for bacteria-infected wound. B. Temperature curves (i) and IR thermal images (ii) of GQDs-COS solutions with different concentrations under light irradiation recorded at different time intervals. Data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). C. Morphology of E. coli and S. aureus by SEM with and without incubation with GQDs-COS under light irradiation. D. (i) Photographs of rat wounds and the corresponding thermographic image after administration of GQDs-COS under light irradiation; (ii) Rat wounds from the four groups at different times during the therapeutic process. H&E and IHC staining of the rat dermal wound of each group are shown.

A. The formulation of PS-NP@(C + I) (i) and particular mechanism of PS-NP@(C + I)-mediated chemo- and PTT/PDT trinary therapy against S. mutans biofilm-induced dental caries (ii). B. (i) Percentage of S. mutans biofilm remaining of different concentrations (i) and different exposing time under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) in various groups (ii). C. CLSM 3D images of S. mutans biofilms in various groups. Red: bacterial cells stained with EB; green: biofilm EPS labeled with FITC-ConA. D. Images of rat teeth after treatment. E. Caries onset and severity of smooth (i) and sulcal surfaces (ii). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A. (i) Illustration of the structure of HMME@Lipo-PMB; (ii) The PDT process of MDR A. baumannii-induced burn infections and wound healing process mediated by HMME@Lipo-PMB. B. The in vitro antibacterial results. (i) Bacterial survival of MDR A. baumannii clinical isolate incubated with different concentrations of HMME, HMME@Lipo, and HMME@Lipo-PMB which irradiated with 96 J cm2 of white light (n = 3); (ii) Bacterial survival of MDR A. baumannii clinical isolate incubated with HMME, HMME@Lipo, and HMME@Lipo-PMB (HMME concentration: 5 μM) which irradiated with different energy doses of light (n = 3). C. In vivo treatment efficacy and wound healing effects of HMME@Lipo-PMB-mediated aPDT for burn infections induced by MDR A. baumannii (i) Photographs of the burn infection wound after different treatments over a 12-day period; (ii) Wound healing rates after different treatments on Day 6 and Day 12 (n = 9).

A. Illustration of Van-Lipo(IR780)-PFC for the targeted aPDT against MRSA infection. B. In vitro anti-MRSA activity of Van-Lipo(IR780)-PFC. (i) MRSA colony growth after different treatments with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1.0 W cm−2, 5 min); group treated with TSB served as the control; (ii) Bacteria enumeration of the experiment in (i) (n = 3, mean ± sd); (iii) Visualization of MRSA colony growth (top) after treatment with Van-Lipo(IR780)-PFC combined with various amounts of sMT under NIR irradiation. C. In vivo anti-MRSA activity of Van-Lipo(IR780)-PFC (i) Secondary MRSA infection and the visualization of MRSA burden; (ii) the lesion sizes analysis of (i); (iii) the corresponding bacterial counts of (i).

A. Illustration of the preparation and PDT/CO antimicrobial mechanism of SCH nano-antibiotics against MRSA infection. B. (i) Concentration variation of the released CO from SGQDs-CORM during white light irradiation (30 mW cm−2) for 360 s; (ii) Representative TEM image of MRSA after treatment with SCH for 4 h under white light irradiation (30 mW cm−2) for different periods (scale bar: 100 nm). C. Antibacterial abilities in vitro. (i) Representative SEM images of MRSA after different treatments; (ii) CFU reduction rate of MRSA after different treatments. D. Antibacterial and would healing performance of SCH in vivo. (i) Representative photographs of wound area on day 1, 3, 6 and 9 after different treatments. Numerical simulation was performed to show the shrink of would infection area (blue and pink region represents the healthy and infection site, respectively); (ii) corresponding quantification of wound closure rate of mice on day 9 after different treatments. head.ce:title: Functional nanomaterials as photosensitizers or delivery systems for antibacterial photodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection is a global health problem that closely related to various diseases threatening human life. Although antibiotic therapy has been the mainstream treatment method for various bacterial infectious diseases for decades, the increasing emergence of bacterial drug resistance has brought enormous challenges to the application of antibiotics. Therefore, developing novel antibacterial strategies is of great importance. By producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) with photosensitizers (PSs) under light irradiation, antibacterial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) has emerged as a non-invasive and promising approach for treating bacterial infections without causing drug resistance. However, the insufficient therapeutic penetration, poor hydrophilicity, and poor biocompatibility of traditional PSs greatly limit the efficacy of aPDT. Recently, studies have found that nanomaterials with characteristics of favorable photocatalytic activity, surface plasmonic resonance, easy modification, and high drug loading capacity can improve the therapeutic efficacy of aPDT. In this review, we aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of the mechanism of nanomaterials-mediated aPDT and summarize the representative nanomaterials in aPDT, either as PSs or carriers for PSs. In addition, the combination of advanced nanomaterials-mediated aPDT with other therapies, including targeted therapy, gas therapy, and multidrug resistance (MDR) therapy, is reviewed. Also, the concerns and possible solutions of nanomaterials-based aPDT are discussed. Overall, this review may provide theoretical basis and inspiration for the development of nanomaterials-based aPDT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections accompanied by biofilm formation are closely related to various diseases that threaten human life, such as chronic wounds, sepsis, and pneumonia, thus posing a great challenge to global public health [1,2]. Since the first discovery of penicillin in 1928, antibiotic therapy has been developed rapidly and has become the mainstream therapy for infectious diseases for decades [3]. However, the overuse of antibiotics and the rapid mutation of bacteria have caused the emergence of bacterial drug resistance, limiting the antibacterial efficacy of traditional antibiotics and even leading to treatment failure [4–6]. Hence, it is significant to develop safe and efficient treatment strategies independent on antibiotics for bacterial infections [7].

With broad antibacterial spectrum, antibacterial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) has shown great potential in treating bacterial infections, especially in multidrug resistance (MDR) treatment [8]. As a widely applied method for cancer therapy, photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive therapeutic approach that relies on the interaction between photosensitive agents and light to induce local chemical damage [9,10]. It is reported that reactive oxygen species (ROS), the end-product in PDT, can damage biofilm and bacteria metabolism (e.g., proteins, nucleic acid, lipid, etc.) without triggering drug resistance [11]. As one of the key components in aPDT, photosensitizers (PSs) can utilize light and oxygen to generate ROS, therefore determining the efficiency of antibacterial activity [12]. However, the current performance of PSs remains unsatisfied, such as poor water insolubility and insufficient therapeutic penetration [13]. Developing suitable PSs and achieving sufficient therapeutic penetration have great significance in improving the antibacterial activity of aPDT [14].

Recently, nanomaterials have attracted great attention in various biomedical fields such as drug delivery, anti-tumor therapy, and antibacterial therapy [15–20]. Due to their easy modification, high drug loading efficacy, and favorable photocatalytic activity, various nanomaterials functioning as PSs or PSs' carriers are regarded as promising antibacterial agents to enhance aPDT [21,22]. Specifically, functional nanomaterials like gold nanomaterials and carbon dots have unique physiochemical properties, such as surface plasmon resonance, making them a new generation of PS with better capability to produce ROS [23,24]. While the high loading efficacy of non-functional nanomaterials, typically represented by liposomes, endows them with the ability to carry PSs, therefore improving the water solubility of PSs as well as prolonging their working time [25]. In addition, nanomaterials-based aPDT can perform multi-antimicrobial abilities by functional modification of nano-delivery systems [26]. Conclusively, the fast development of nanomaterials has brought PDT with enhanced efficacy and reduced side effects.

In this review, we aim to provide a systematic review on the potential of nanomaterials-based PSs and delivery systems for antibacterial photodynamic therapy (Fig. 1 ). First, we briefly summarized the roles of nanomaterials in aPDT (either as PSs or PSs' carriers) and detailly analyzed the underlying mechanism of nanomaterials-mediated aPDT. Then the representative nanomaterials as PSs (noble metal nanomaterials, metal oxide nanomaterials, and carbon-based nanomaterials) and carriers for PSs (polymer-based nanomaterials, upconversion nanoparticles, and liposomes) for aPDT were introduced, thereby providing an insight into different functions of nanomaterials in nano-PSs complex. Furthermore, we reviewed the combination of advanced nanomaterials-mediated aPDT with other therapies, including targeted therapy, gas therapy, and MDR therapy. Finally, the concerns and possible solutions of nanomaterials-based aPDT were discussed.

As we mentioned above, PSs serve as aPDT mediators that play vital roles in producing ROS by utilizing light and oxygen. Ideally, PSs should possess following features: 1) sensitive to light of specific wavelengths; 2) stable in the dark; 3) good biosafety and biocompatibility [27]. However, traditional PSs have poor hydrophilicity and could only reach an insufficient penetration into bacterial biofilms, which largely hindered aPDT efficacy [28]. Versatile nanomaterials have brought bright future for advanced aPDT due to their easy modification, high drug loading efficacy, favorable photocatalytic activity, etc. [29,30]. By assembling PSs with nanomaterials, the therapeutic effects of aPDT can be largely enhanced. Briefly, nanomaterials assist PSs in two roles. Non-functional nanomaterials, such as liposomes and polymer-based nanoparticles, can serve as carriers for PSs, improving their hydrophilicity and local accumulation [31,32]. Functional nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs), nanoclusters, and graphene-based nanomaterials, are a new generation of PSs that can achieve better ROS productivity and deeper penetration [33,34]. Furthermore, both functional and non-functional nanomaterials can show additional assistance to aPDT with their unique features. For example, upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can convert near-infrared (NIR) light to ultraviolet (UV) light for a safer PSs activation, avoiding tissue damage in therapeutic environment [35]. Silver nanoparticles can release hypertoxic silver ion (Ag+) to kill bacteria [36]. Two-dimension graphene oxide can cut bacteria cell membrane by mechanical force with its sharp edge [37]. Moreover, the easy modification of nanomaterials enables aPDT to be integrated with other therapy such as target therapy and gas therapy, making it multifunctional [38,39]. Conclusively, nanomaterials have played a significant auxiliary role in enhancing the performance of PSs, thereby endowing aPDT with great potential as an alternative to antibiotics therapy.

Generally, the antibacterial mechanisms of nanomaterials-based aPDT are synergetic. First and foremost, excessive ROS (such as singlet oxygen, superoxide anions, and hydroxyl radicals) induced by PSs (Fig. 2 ) plays a key role in antibacterial activities of PDT [40]. As shown in Fig. 2, the ground state PSs (S0) absorb photons and turns to the excited singlet state (S1) under UV/NIR irradiation. This state undergoes intersystem crossing, forming the excited triplet state PSs (T1) that can interact with oxygen through two pathways: in pathway 1, ROS is generated by electronic exchange from the excited PSs; in pathway 2, the triplet ground state oxygen (3O2) directly reacts with PSs to generate excited singlet oxygen (1O2). Eventually, activated PSs returns to the initial state and continue to generate ROS in circle [41]. As reported, excessive ROS can inhibit bacterial activities by damaging bacterial cell walls, cell membranes, proteins, and nucleic acids. Typically, the oxidative damage caused by ROS can lead to covalent modifications that destabilize and inactivate bacterial proteins, especially those proteins with the amino acids cysteine (Cys) and methionine (Met) [42]. As such, bacterial adhesion can be arrested in aPDT by ROS denaturing adhesins, which are the crucial proteins for bacterial adhesion [11].

In addition to the cytotoxicity of ROS, some nanomaterials also exhibit natural antibacterial activity due to their physiochemical properties. For example, due to the large surface of the metal nanocrystals, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) can release hypertoxic Ag+ in oxidizing microenvironment. According to reports, Ag+ can directly destroy bacterial cell walls, DNA, and enzymes through chemical binding to inactivate bacterial activity [43]. Moreover, the two-dimensional lamellar graphene oxide nanosheets can damage bacterial cell walls through mechanical and chemical properties such as the sharp edges effect and membrane wrapping, as well as abilities to generate ROS and induce charge transfer [44]. Interestingly, aPDT also exhibits specific mechanisms against aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, depending on the type and depth of bacterial infections. For superficial infections induced by aerobic bacteria, PSs can consume the oxygen in bacterial microenvironment by pathway 2, thus limiting the growth of aerobic bacteria [45]. While for deep infections induced by anaerobic bacteria, traditional PSs are difficult to accumulate in the deep tissue due to the insufficient penetration of UV light [46]. Fortunately, nanomaterials-based PSs can solve this problem by improving PSs' local accumulation and utilizing NIR light which have a deeper penetration [47]. Therefore, nanomaterials-based aPDT shows broad-spectrum and favorable antibacterial ability in most bacterial infections, even for MDR infection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Representative nanomaterials as PSs for aPDT

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanomaterials, including silver (Ag), gold (Au) and six platinum (Pt) group metals, have shown broad-spectrum antibacterial properties with abilities of destabilizing bacterial membranes, inducing ROS, and disturbing biomolecules such as DNA, enzymes and membrane proteins [48–50]. Moreover, it has been verified that noble metal nanomaterials possess inherent photosensitivity with their nano-size and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect, endowing them potential to act as photosensitizing agents for aPDT [51,52]. Among all noble metal nanomaterials, gold and silver nanomaterials are the minority whose surface plasmonic vibrational absorptions located within the visible light wavelength range, making them the most extensively studied materials [53,54]. In this part, we mainly discuss the gold and silver nanomaterials-based aPDT and their methodology, mechanism, and antibacterial results.

Nowadays, silver nanomaterials have been widely used in various industries, including medicine, textiles, packaging, and electronics due to their excellent bactericidal and antimicrobial effects [55–57]. As reported, the antimicrobial effect of silver nanomaterials is mainly attributed to the bacterial toxicity of Ag+, including inactivating proteins, DNAs, as well as destroying cell walls [58]. While silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) only possess slight photosensitivity, more studies focused on their SPR effects on ROS yield enhancement in aPDT. For example, Zhang et al. demonstrated a nanoplatform that decorates AgNPs on porphyrinic porous coordination network (PCN) and further coats it with neutrophil membrane (NM) to achieve on-demand activation and release of Ag+ by controlling oxidation conditions [59]. This results in selective accumulation at the infectious site due to the inflammatory targeting ability of NM. Under NIR irradiation at infected tissues, PCN locally generates singlet oxygen (1O2), enabling AgNPs to release cytotoxic Ag+ to kill bacteria. Moreover, the incorporation of AgNPs can enhance the efficiency of PCN to produce 1O2 due to the SPR effect. This controlled and enhanced PDT, resulting from the interactions between AgNPs and PCN, provides a possible solution for efficient pathogen elimination. Likewise, Wei et al. mounted Graphix (the PS), which was encapsuled in haemoglobin, on 10 nm AgNPs as nano-PSs for targeted aPDT against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) infection [60]. Under irradiation, GaPpIX received free electrons from nearby AgNPs and produced more 1O2 than GaPpIX alone, exhibiting an encouraging antibacterial effects. Additionally, it was observed that GaHb adsorption on 10 nm AgNPs is significantly more effective than on 40 nm AgNPs or 10 nm AuNPs, which suggested that both size and plasmon-resonant coupling play crucial roles in enhancing aPDT. Besides, silver nanomaterials such as AgNPs and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) can also assist PSs as carriers for enhanced aPDT [61,62]. It's reported that RB (a PS) loaded onto AgNCs was more effective than either AgNCs or RB alone in inhibiting S. aureus and Escherichia coli (E. coli), indicating a synergy between ROS and Ag+ ions from photoexcited AgNCs/RB [63].

Gold nanomaterials are tiny gold particles with a diameter of 1-100 nm, where gold nanorods (AuNRs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are the most popular in the medical research [64]. Gold nanorods have attracted considerable interest for their unique optical and electronic properties depending on their shape, size and aspect ratio [65]. Through the hot-electron injection mechanism, plasmonic gold nanorods can effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of PSs in the visible and near-infrared regions. Additionally, they can collect more longitudinal SPR energy through the vertices at both ends. Such mechanism provides a possibility for NIR-II light triggered PDT, which compensates for the shallow therapeutic depth and the low ROS production of traditional PDT [66]. In a study, Wang et al. designed the hybrid plasmonic nanodumbbells for ideal NIR-II light induced aPDT, which was composed of AuNRs (the middle), CdSexSy (the PS, the two ends) and Ag (doping) (Fig. 3 ) [67]. In this nanodumbbells, plasmonic AuNRs extend the photocatalytic activity to NIR-II regions, while the effective hot-electrons injection and a separation of electron holes caused by AuNRs and doping Ag provide great convenience for the production of ROS. Interestingly, the Se4 group shows the best antibacterial ability as well as antibiofilm ability with no bacteria existing both in vitro and in vivo, according to the value of OD600 nm, live/dead cell staining kits, scanning electron microscope (SEM) experiment. This suggests that plasmonic AuNRs can provide an alternative strategy for the NIR-II light induced aPDT. Moreover, AuNPs are favorable photothermal agents as well with applications in antibacterial photothermal/photodynamic therapy (PTT/PDT) [68–70]. Development of AuNP-based nanocomposites combined with PSs can improve the effectiveness of both PDT and combined therapies. Gold nanoclusters are relatively stable nanostructures made up of a few gold atoms. Due to their ultrasmall size closed to molecules (<3 nm), AuNCs show more active photosensitivity and photocatalytic ability than AuNRs, and can possibly emerge as a new type of PSs [71]. For example, Xie et al. developed deoxyribonuclease (DNase)-functionalized AuNCs as an efficient phototherapy agent to kill Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, especially dispersing the surrounding biofilms [72]. After DNase hydrolysis of the extracellular polymeric substance matrix of biofilms, ROS and heat generated from AuNCs directly damaged the exposed bacteria under 808 nm laser irradiation. This combination of enzymolysis, PDT, and PTT can effectively remove biofilms, achieving a dispersion rate of up to 80 % and kill approximately 90 % of the shielded bacteria. However, while AuNCs' functions are dependent on the chemical composition at the atomic and molecular levels, the stable and mature preparation methods are still needed to be improved, which may be the direction of AuNCs' development in the future [73]. Apart from the nanomaterials mentioned above, some AuNPs are also used for antibacterial PDT [74,75].

Being more accessible and less expensive than noble metal nanomaterials, metal oxide nanomaterials possess a greater potential in the translation to market application [76]. For the last decades, these oxide nanoparticles have been applied in various fields including catalytic medicine [77], air purification [78], green degradation [79], and anticancer therapy [80]. Moreover, a diversity of metal oxide nanoparticles, such as TiO2, Fe3O4, and ZnO have been proved to exhibit selective toxicity on bacteria [81]. However, the application of metal oxide nanoparticles in antibacterial PDT is rarely summarized. In this part, we mainly focus on four typical metal oxide nanoparticles in aPDT, with each unique property discussed.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanomaterials, as one of the most important inorganic functional nanomaterials, have caught researchers' eyes since last decade. The long-term stability, favorable biocompatibility and strong mechanical properties grant them privileges of tremendous applications such as antibacterial/antitumor medicine, UV protection, air cleaner and solar cell industry [82,83]. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanomaterials contributes to fight against antibiotic resistant bacteria as well [84]. For example, Wang et al. designed a TiO2/TiO2−x metasurface with potent NIR-responsive antibacterial activity on a Ti alloy implant by a newly invented topochemical conversion-based alkaline–acid bidirectional hydrothermal method (aaBH) (Fig. 4 ) [85]. Under low-power NIR light irradiation, the metasurface generated 1O2 and hydroxyl radical (OH), resulting in a potent antibacterial activity. As the results showed, the antimicrobial rates against E. coli and S. aureus in vitro were 96.88 % and 97.56 %, respectively while the antimicrobial rate against S. aureus was 91.8 % in vivo. In another study, Huang et al. synthesized a gold and titanium oxide-based photocatalyst nanocomposite (TiOx–Au NCs) with a one-step, green, and low-temperature synthesis method to realize high photocatalytic microbial inactivation [86]. In vitro, ROS caused damage to the bacterial membrane, leading to the inactivation of E. coli and MRSA. Additionally, collagen induction, keratinocyte migration, and neovascularization were detected in rats with MRSA-infected wounds. However, the practical applications of TiO2 are hindered by its suboptimal dispersibility, and weak photocatalytic activity under visible light [87]. With the great chemical stability, and electronic acceptor properties, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) may provide a solution to address the above issue. For example, Liang et al. loaded TiO2 on rGO for improved photodynamic performance [88]. The modification of TiO2 broadens their antibacterial functions. As a result, rGO enhanced the dispersity of TiO2 in water as well as ROS production under visible light conditions.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) NPs show significant growth inhibition of a wide spectrum of bacterial species, including S. aureus, E. coli, Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis) etc. [89]. Generally, their antibacterial mechanisms are attributed to the liberation of antimicrobial Zn2+ ions and photocatalytic ROS formation. The traditional synthesis of ZnO NPs mainly includes physical and chemical methods, but threatens environment due to those toxic and expensive organic solvents [90]. Green chemical synthesis is a promising alternative to solve this problem. For example, R.G. Chaudhary et al. fabricated ZnO NPs with the assistance of a plant called Bauhinia racemose as a biosynthesized method [91]. Moreover, biosynthesized ZnO NPs show an enhanced antibacterial activity against Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae) during lunar eclipse than normal days. However, pure ZnO NPs usually exhibit relatively low photoenergy conversion efficiency due to the low charge separation efficiency and the rapid recombination of charge carriers. Novel metal nanomaterials can assist ZnO NPs to improve ROS production with their SPR effects. In a study, Wu et al. designed Ag/Ag@AgCl/ZnO hybrid nanostructures which was embedded in a hydrogel for advanced photocatalytic antibacterial efficacy [92]. To test whether Ag/Ag@AgCl assisted ZnO to generate ROS, identification of ROS was detected. Results showed that the electron spin resonance (ESR) relative intensity generated from the Ag/Ag@AgCl/ZnO nanocomposite hydrogel is significantly higher than that of the pure ZnO nanocomposite hydrogel, indicating that deposition of Ag/Ag@AgCl boosts photogeneration of the hydroxyl radical and singlet oxygen. Under 20 min exposure to simulated visible light, this hydrogel nanostructures kills 95.95 % of E. coli and 98.49 % of S. aureus in vitro, and the release of Zn2+ accelerated wound healing in vivo. Apart from modification of ZnO NPs, different synthesis also affects their photocatalytic antibacterial abilities [93].

Other metal oxides like MnO2 and superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs can also be used in antibacterial PDT. While a hypoxia microenvironment can largely hinder the antibacterial efficiency of PDT, MnO2 can relieve the hypoxia pressure in bacterial microenvironment by converting endogenous H2O2 to O2 [94]. For example, Wang et al. designed a MnO2-based nanoplatform (Ce6@MnO2-PEG NPs) for improved aPDT [95]. With mass O2 released under MnO2 catalysis, chlorine e6 (Ce6, the PS) could generate maximum ROS under LED irradiation. Consequently, over 80 % of S. aureus was suppressed in vitro, and the abscess was relieved and healed. Moreover, Mn2+ from MnO2 strengthened immunological memory during body defense, further enhancing aPDT efficacy. Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs), such as Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, have attracted tremendous attention as innovative PDT agents due to their outstanding magnetism [96]. For example, Balhaddad et al. fabricated a SPION-based nanoparticle with PSs loading [97]. Under the magnetic field force, motivated versatile SPIONs could penetrate deeper into the biofilms derived from Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans) and saliva. As the PSs accumulated at dental caries lesions, it could produce cytotoxic ROS in the local area, hence enhancing antibacterial efficacy of 4.5 log10 biofilm reduction and reducing systematic side effects.

Carbon-based nanomaterials (CBN) have emerged over the last few years as important agents for antibacterial applications [98]. Generally, CBN possess instinctive antibacterial abilities, such as inducing mechanical damage, oxidative stress, and photothermal/photodynamic effects [99,100]. Moreover, by modulating physicochemical parameters (size, shape, surface charge etc.), their antibacterial efficiency differs as well [101]. Such favorable modifiability endows CBN to act as both PSs and PSs' carriers in antibacteiral PDT. Based on dimensions, carbon-based nanomaterials (CBN) are classified into 3 categories: 0D CBN, such as carbon dots and fullerene; 1D CBN, such as carbon nanotubes (CNT); and 2D graphene-based nanomaterials (GBN), such as pristine graphene, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [102].

Carbon dots (CDots), which are defined as a class of spherical carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles with a typical size of 10 nm or below, have emerged to represent a new platform for visible/natural light-activated antimicrobial agents [103]. Compared to traditional PSs, CDots are more safer PSs with nontoxicity, quantum confinement effect and high drug loading capacity [104,105]. For example, Wei et al. synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene in ethanol solvent and estimated its ability as PSs in vitro antibacterial photodynamic inactivation of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [106]. Through pathway 2, CQDs generated 1O2 under visible light, resulting in inactivation of both E. coli and S. aureus up. While the generated ROS can reduce the photostability of the CDots itself, a balance between the two parts is crucial for CDots' modification. To solve this issue, Das et al. conjugated bovine serum albumin (BSA) on CDots covalently [107]. After the amine functionality of BSA derivatized the surface carboxyl functional groups of CDots, ROS generation increased largely. This is the first report that demonstrates the possibility of tuning ROS generation from CDots which provided a novel strategy for antibacterial PDT. Besides, functionalizing CDots' surface for desired microbial adhesion and interactions are another strategy for enhanced aPDT. For example, Mei et al. modified positive-charge chitosan oligosaccharide (COS) on GQDs for target aPDT (Fig. 5 ) [108]. With an accurate release at infected sites, GQDs-COS exhibited the photodynamic/photothermal antibacterial effect on E. coli and S. aureus up to 80 % in vitro. While COS endowed GQDs positive charge to easily adhere to bacteria by electrostatic interaction, excessive generation of 1O2 and heat can irreversibly damage bacterial cells by disrupting their cytomembranes and leaking cytoplasm according to SEM. Furthermore, in vivo studies indicated that the administration of GQDs-COS followed by light irradiation could effectively promote wound healing.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are tubular materials made of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal pattern to form a sheet of graphene, which is then rolled into a hollow tube that consisting of a single or multiple layers. Owing to their sp2-hybridized carbon surfaces and large surface area, CNTs have a high loading capacity for drug delivery and modifications [109]. While CNTs can also applied in antibacterial applications, the lack of functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups on the surface limits their antimicrobial activity [110]. Functionally modified CNTs is a useful strategy to enhance their performance. For example, Asad Syed et al. carboxyl-functionalized CNTs with malachite green conjugated (MGCNT) [111]. Compared to free dye, treatment with MGCNT exhibited improved phototoxicity against bacteria, with greater ROS production. Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) and S. aureus biofilms were considerably reduced to 60.20 ± 2.48 % and 67.59 ± 3.53 %, respectively, while improved biofilm inhibition, exopolysaccharide inhibition, and reduced cell viability were detected.

Graphene-based nanomaterials, such as GO and rGO, have also caught great interest in antibacterial phototherapy [112,113]. Plane GO nanomaterial is an oxide derivative of graphene, which can be synthesized by Staudenmaier method, Brodie method, or Hummers method [114–116]. With good photosensitivity and mechanical antibacterial abilities (such as sharp edge cutting and cells entrapment), GO is a potential PDT and PTT agent for broad-spectrum antibacterial therapy [117]. In a study, María et al. synthesized two sizes of GO and verified their bimodal effects of PDT and PTT in the destruction of S. aureus and E. coli [118]. However, the author didn't find the different antibacterial effects of these two sizes GO, which contradicted the facts that sizes of NPs may affect their physiochemical properties [119]. In fact, GO are more commonly used as PSs' carriers or photothermal agents in aPDT. Enriched with hydrophilic groups like -COOH and -OH, GO possess a high feasibility to load hydrophobic materials [120]. For example, Zhang et al. incorporated GO, AIEgen (a hydrophobic PS), and biocompatible bovine serum albumin (BSA) into a nanoparticle (GO-BSA-AIE NPs) for dual-mode phototherapy in the treatment of antibiotic resistance bacteria [121]. Uniting AIE with GO achieved synergistic PTT/PDT outcomes and promote enhanced bacterial ablation. Irradiated with daylight and NIR, GO-BSA-AIE NPs caused over 99 % bacteria (both S. aureus and E. coli) viability loss. Moreover, the addition of BSA improves NGO-BSA-AIE NPs stability and excellently controllable biocompatibility. The functionalized GO and graphitic carbon nitride were designed as dual-phototherapy agents for PDT/PTT in another study [122]. Obtained through GO reduction reaction, rGO has similar physiochemical properties as GO, but a stronger photothermal ability [123,124]. In a study, rGO was grafted with curcumin (Cur, the PS) as an anti-inflammatory and photosensitive drug for endodontic infection treatment [125]. Compared to traditional drugs (e.g., NaClO), rGO-Cur preformed a stronger inhibition on E. faecalis biofilms after LED irradiation, with the additional heat damage and ROS cytotoxicity. In another study, Tan et al. fabricated rGO/Ag nanocomposite for E. coli photo-disinfection [126]. Under mild NIR irradiation (0.3 W/cm2 for 10 min), the rGO/Ag nanocomposite demonstrated enhanced synergetic antibacterial activity through endogenic ROS and photothermal effects, with nearly 100 % of E. coli were killed.

With inherent photosensitivity, nanomaterials can directly play as PSs for aPDT, which can also produce cytotoxic ROS after light activation. Moreover, they perform a better antibacterial ability than traditional PSs with special properties. Here we summarize some typical examples of nanomaterials as PSs for aPDT in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Representative nanomaterials as carriers for aPDT

ce:section.ce:para: As a versatile class of nanocarriers that allows controllable drug delivery, polymer-based nanomaterials stand out among various nanotherapeutics in antibacterial PDT [127,128]. Among the natural polymers, chitosan-based nanoparticles have emerged as one of the most popular biopolymers used in nanomedicine due to their outstanding merits such as excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, low toxicity, hemostatic and antimicrobial activity, simple preparation method, and easy functionalization [129]. In a study, Jin et al. constructed a chitosan sponge incorporated with Ce6 and ZnO particles for both rapid bacterial killing and long-term bacterial inhibition for treatment of MDR bacteria-infected wound [130]. When loading in biocompatible chitosan sponge, the photosensitive Ce6 and the antibacterial agent ZnO were able to disperse at lesions controllably and continuously. After illuminated with light for 15 min, the CS-ZnO/Ce6 sponge exhibited excellent bacterial killing ability, of 96 % and 98 % for S. aureus and MRSA, respectively. Moreover, the antibacterial inhibition effect maintained for 4 h after irradiation, further verifying chitosan's ability of controlled release. In vivo, the region of the wound treated with CS-ZnO/Ce6 + Light had recovered by 89 % after 14 days. Similar chitosan-based PSs/drug delivery platforms are well documented [131–133]. To manage efficient drug delivery to the target site, various strategies have been developed in polymer-based nanomaterials by incorporating stimuli-responsive properties (e.g., pH, temperature, or light activation). Polyethylene glycol (PEG), as the most frequently employed hydrophilic, shellforming block in polymeric micelles, evokes great interest in stimuli-responsive aPDT design with its favorable sensitivity to pH [134,135]. For example, Yu et al. constructed a pH-sensitive and self-degradable PEG nanoplatform coloaded with IR870 (the PS) and ciprofloxacin (CIP, the antibiotics) for a promising antibacterial therapy for the efficient prevention of dental caries (Fig. 6 ) [136]. In acidic oral biofilms, PEG nanoplatform collapsed and locally released photosensitive IR870 and bactericidal CIP, inhibiting 95 % S. mutans viability under NIR irradiation. Furthermore, topical oral treatment with nanoplatforms using synergistic PDT, PTT, and antibiotic trinary therapy results in robust biofilm dispersion and effective suppression of severe dental caries in rats. Also, relative studies on PEG-based nanoplatforms loading PSs, such as AgNPs and TPPNH2, are detailly reported in aPDT [69,137].

Recently, polydopamine (PDA) has provided a novel strategy in surface modification with extraordinary adhesiveness, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, strong photothermal conversion capacity and ROS scavenging facility [138]. Studies showed that by loading PSs on PDA, their performance under irradiation is enhanced [139,140]. For instance, Su et al. prepare a compound system of PDA and curcumin (PDA-Cur nanocomposites) with excellent photodynamic and photothermal effects [141]. Compared with free Cur, PDA-Cur had higher efficiency of 1O2 production, followed by an improved antibacterial activity. With mesoporous structures, mesoporous polydopamine nanoparticles (MPDA) provides larger drug loading efficiency. For example, Yuan et al. developed surface modification of Ti implants based on MPDA nanoparticles with RGD peptide immobilization and photosensitizer (ICG) loading [142]. As photothermal material and photosensitizer carrier, MPDA generated additional heat and assisted ICG to produce ROS efficiently. The results in vivo confirmed that the established biofilm on Ti-M/I/RGD implant could be effectively eliminated with an efficiency of 95.4 % under NIR irradiation. In another study, Yuan et al. modified mesoporous PDA with indocyanine green (ICG) via π − π stacking, and further grafted L-arginine (L-Arg) externally as an integrated therapy of NIR-triggered PTT and nitric oxide (NO)-enhanced PDT [143]. Under NIR irradiation, mesoporous PDA displayed a mild photothermal effect, as the elevated low temperature triggered the local release of ICG. Subsequently, bulks of ROS catalyzed the NO release from L-Arg, thereby eradicating 99 % of S. aureus biofilms. Other polymer-based nanomaterials such as poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) [144], poly(glycolic acid) (PGA) [145] are also reported in antibacterial PDT.

Compared to UV light with potential phototoxicity and unsatisfied penetrability, NIR (650–1000 nm) light has brought a bright prospect for a safer aPDT due to its non-invasiveness, biocompatibility, and deeper penetration [146]. However, it's hard for traditional PSs to utilize NIR light directly. Therefore, converting NIR light to UV light for traditional PSs utilization can be a promising strategy to avoid the adverse effects of UV light-induced PDT [147]. UCNPs is a photonic nanomaterial known for anti-stokes shift upconversion luminescence (UCL) [148]. Usually, UNCPs are functionalized with photosensitive agents or bioactive molecules in aPDT practice, which are mostly designed in “core and shell” form [149,150]. By converting NIR light to UV light in the UNCP core, PSs anchored on the surface can be safely activated to generate ROS. For example, Liu et al. loaded rose bengal (RB, a PS) on UNCPs core as an efficient PS to achieve a satisfying bacterial inhibition against Acinetobacter baumannii [151]. After NIR laser (980 nm) irradiation, the high-efficiency energy transfer from UCNPs to the loaded PS allowed NIR-triggered generation of ROS for destroying the bacterial cell membrane. UNCP-based nanocomposite resulted in a decline of 4.72 log10 in bacteria viability in vitro, as well as accelerated wound healing progress in vivo. In addition, UNCPs are also a suitable light-controlled gas-releasing nanoplatform for enhanced integrated aPDT [152]. Zhou et al. established an UCNP-based nanoplatform (POS-UCNPs/ICG) to improve the antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effect of PDT [153]. After UCNPs converted NIR light to UV light, ICG (the PS) and partially oxidized SnS2 (POS, the donor of carbon monoxide (CO) and O2) were activated to generate ROS (reaction I), CO, and O2 (reaction II), respectively. In this regard, the antibacterial effect was enhanced significantly: on the one hand, oxygen-enriched environment accelerated ROS generation in pathway 2; on the other hand, CO performed great anti-inflammatory effect. Moreover, up to 90 % of the infected area in the abscess mouse healed after POS-UCNPs/ICG treatment. In another work, the combination of PDT and NO antibacterial therapy also achieved 99 % bacteria inhibition with NO donors and bacteria targeting molecules on the UCNP-core [154].

Conclusively, nanomaterials-based delivery system has brought bright prospect as PSs carriers for aPDT, including homodispersing PSs, improving biocompatibility of PSs, controllably releasing PSs and functionalizing PSs. Here, we list a few examples of nanomaterials as carriers for aPDT in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials-based aPDT combined with other therapies

ce:section.ce:para: Although aPDT provides great therapeutic potential in antibacterial filed, the lack of bacteria-specific target can lead to off-target heat and ROS generation, thereby harming healthy cells and compromising treatment efficacy. Targeted aPDT, assisted by nanomaterials-based PSs modified with cationic groups or corresponding bacterial ligands, brings advanced antibacterial therapy a bright future [160]. Through reaching the target bacteria and generating ROS locally, nanomaterials-based PSs greatly improves aPDT efficacy and decreases damage [161,162].

After exposure to body fluid, nanoparticles can absorb proteins like albumin and form negatively-charged protein corona outside [163,164]. These nanomaterials are difficult to interact with bacteria cells, which possess negative charge due to quantities of teichoic acid and lipopolysaccharide on cell walls. Fortunately, cationic surface modified nanomaterials are able to aggregate at bacterial biofilms locally through electrostatic attraction and conduct ROS cytotoxicity. In this regard, Chen et al. conjugated three cationic groups with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) NPs as a bacteria membrane-anchoring PS [165]. Through both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, membrane-anchoring AIE could interact with MRSA biofilms effectively and reduce the vitality of MRSA to 3.2 % after 400-700 nm laser irradiation. Similar cationic modification was applied in chitosan-based aPDT as well [132]. By loading Ce6 on positively charged chitosan covalently, nanocomposite performed high antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus under 660 nm laser irradiation. In another work, Jin et al. fabricated iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) with chitosan coating (WCS) and photosensitive Ce6 for advanced anti-MRSA biofilm PDT [131]. Through strong electrostatic interactions, Ce6@WCS-IONP targeted MRSA selectively and penetrated in biofilm deeply. Under NIR irradiation, MRSA biofilm ablated remarkably in vitro (7.1 log unit reduction), and infectious wound healed in vivo.

Inspired by tumor-targeted mechanism, PSs modified with specific bacterial ligands have also shown great potential in targeted aPDT [166,167]. Giving that boronic acid can bind to cis-o-dihydroxy structure on bacterial walls, Chen et al. integrated bacterial-binding boronic acid ligand and porphyrin into one single metal−organic framework (MOF) to boost targeted aPDT efficacy [168]. The results showed that MOF-based nanocomplex with target ligand possessed extraordinary antibacterial efficiency, which was 10–20 times higher than that of undecorated groups. In addition, as mature antibacterial agents, antibiotics can benefit targeted aPDT as well [22]. Ciprofloxacin, a quinolone drug, can inhibit bacterial metabolism by disturbing key enzymes in DNA replication (DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV) [169]. Yu et al. designed a pH-response PEG-based nanoplatform with ciprofloxacin and IR870 coating inside [136]. As surrounding pH increased, the detachable PEG could release ciprofloxacin and IR870 at deeper biofilms to target bacteria and produce ROS in situ. By interacting with terminal D-alanyl-D-alanine on Gram-positive bacteria, vancomycin is an effective antibiotic against MRSA [170]. In another study, Chen et al. developed a multi-functional liposome nanoplatform consisting of vancomycin grafted outside, IR870 and perfluorocarbon (PFC, an oxygen-giver) embedded inside (Fig. 8 ) [171]. After vancomycin recognized MRSA, IR870 produced overheat and ROS around the target bacteria, and PFC released ample oxygen to accelerate aPDT process. It's worth noting that this strategy cannot be reduced to the simple combination of PTT/PDT and antivirulence. MRSA secreted toxin (sMT)-triggered pore formation on the liposomal layer could result in the accelerated release of IR780 and PFC-encapsulated oxygen. Therefore, the therapeutic effects of PTT/PDT are localized to regions with a high density of MRSA. Under NIR irradiation, local increases in temperature and ROS effectively kill the bacteria up to 100 %. In addition, once captured by the nanoplatform, sMTs could no long damage host cells and be processed by resident immune cells to promote multi-antigen immunity that protects against secondary MRSA infections.

Gas therapy, such as carbon monoxide (CO) therapy, nitric oxide (NO) therapy, and O2 therapy, has gained broad attention in antibacterial therapy due to the tiny size and favorable diffusion of gas [172,173]. Commonly, functional gases are released from precursor gas molecules after stimulation, then conducting therapeutic effects [174]. However, the uncontrollable local release of gas poses threat to body safety, largely limiting its clinical applications. Gas-generating nanoplatforms are novel strategies that can control the delivery of bioactive gases within a safe and effective concentration range [175]. By combining with aPDT, gas-generating nanoplatforms are able to exhibit stronger antibacterial ability.

Carbon monoxide (CO), as a necessary biological signaling molecule, shows versatile antibacterial abilities, such as cutting off bacteria ATP supplies, suppressing bacteria cellular respiration, and activating host immune response [176]. CORM-401, as the most common CO-releasing molecule, has been applied widely in CO-based antibacterial PDT. For example, Ma et al. developed a PDT-driven CO-controllable release system by conjugating Ce6 and encapsulating CORM-401 into peptide dendrimer nanogels [177]. By utilizing Ce6-generated H2O2, CORM-401 could release CO without affecting 1O2 generation, enhancing synergetic antibacterial effect. In another study, Liu et al. loaded CORM-401 covalently and hyaluronic acid (HA) electrostatically on single-layered graphene quantum dots (SGQDs), abbreviated as SCH, for anti-MRSA infection (Fig. 9 ) [178]. After MRSA-secreted hyaluronidase cleaved HA (Step I), MRSA biofilms were directly exposed to sharp-edge nanosheets SGQDs-CORM, which then cut bacteria cell membrane and localized in MRSA (Step II). Under irradiation, bulks of 1O2 were generated and accumulated (Step III), which then triggered CORM-401 to release CO for ancillary gas therapy (Step IV). It's further demonstrated that MRSA had a selective cellular uptake of SCH, and the nanoplatform showed good biocompatibility toward both normal somatic cells and non-targeted bacteria. While as high as 83.9 % of MRSA cells were inhibited in the positive control group of vancomycin, the group of “SCH + light” exhibited a complete MRSA inhibition (over 99 % of death) due to the strong photodynamic/CO gas therapy. In vitro, permanent healing of the wound infection was achieved with “SCH + light” treatment, and the wound closure rate of >95 % was achieved on day 9.

Nitric oxide also has inherent antibacterial ability via various mechanisms, such as causing lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, and protein damage [179]. Integrated with NO therapy, aPDT performs enhanced antibacterial efficacy [180]. On the one hand, NO relieves hypoxia microenvironment in biofilm by consuming glutathione; on the other hand, NO reacts with ROS to generate reactive nitrogen species, which conducts stronger peroxidation and sterilization functions than ROS [181]. Based on this, Hu et al. constructed a pH-sensitive polymer assembled with NO and Ce6 for anti-MRSA PDT/NO therapy, resulting in 99 % loss of MRSA viability under 660 nm irradiation [182]. For advanced anti-P. aeruginosa therapy, Wu et al. encapsulated photosensitive sodium nitroprusside (SNP, prodrug of NO) into MOF, and coated PS (Au) and target ligands (HOOC-PEG5000-Mal) outside [183]. By targeting bacterial type IV pili, these nanoparticles selectively adhered to P. aeruginosa. Afterwards, both NO and ROS were produced under irradiation, causing physical damage, nitrosation, and oxidative stress to kill bacteria.

As reported, the hypoxia microenvironment in biofilms is a very important reason for antimicrobial resistance, which has significantly limited the efficacy of aPDT [184]. To solve this issue, liquid perfluorohexane (PFH, an oxygen carrier), which is first applied to tumor-hypoxia therapy, is also used for bacteria-hypoxia therapy [185]. For example, Zhou et al. developed a mesoporous carbon nanoplatform (CIL@ICG/PFH@O2) loading with liquid PFH and ICG for NIR-triggered PTT/PDT synergistic antimicrobial therapy [186]. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, the local temperature increased rapidly, which then promoted the gasification of PFH and significantly accelerated the release of O2 from nanoplatform, rapidly activating the PDT effect of ICG. As the PTT/PDT bactericidal effects showed, the survival rates of MRSA decreased sharply to <1 % with the treatment of CIL@ICG/PFH@O2 and NIR irradiation. Moreover, it could eliminate MRSA-wound infection under NIR irradiation, and the released oxygen can increase collagen deposition, epithelial tissue formation and blood vessel formation to promote wound healing while enhancing the PDT effect. In another work, Wang et al. encapsuled PFH and IR780 in liposomes as self-sufficient oxygen antibacterial nanoparticles [187]. Similarly, PFH induced more production of ROS, which suppressed the viability of E. coli and S. aureus to over 99 % and caused strong bacterial deformation.

While MDR infections have largely hindered the efficacy of traditional antibiotic therapy, nanomaterials-based aPDT provides an alternative way to treat MDR bacterial infections given the following reasons. First, nanomaterials can enhance the performance of PSs, and therefore lead to a significant improved ROS antibacterial outcome without MDR development. Especially, NIR-triggered aPDT with nano-platform provides a safer, deeper, and stronger therapeutic efficacy against MDR bacteria. Second, versatile modifications and loadings on nanomaterials/nanoparticles enabled specialized design of antimicrobial agents contributing to aPDT, achieving accurate and efficient antibacterial effects even under a low drug concentration, thus alleviating any dosage-associated toxicities. Third, by combination with synergic therapies such as targeting bacteria, antibiotics etc., it could boost each other's antibacterial property, thereby minimizing the antimicrobial treatment's off-site effects. This will help to enhance the patient compliance to the treatment and prevent the relapse of infection. Lastly, unlike other antibacterial therapy needing time to take effect, PDT could kill bacteria within seconds to minutes by causing oxidative stress when illuminated.

Basically, there are two ways to improve the antimicrobial efficacy against MDR infections in nanomaterials-based aPDT. One strategy is to improve performance of nanomaterials/PSs, therefore enhancing ROS yield and conducting a stronger damage toward MDR bacteria. For example, Kuo et al. modified GQDs with nitrogen dopants and primary amine molecules (amino-N-GQDs) for impressive two-photon PS which had a superior ability to generate ROS compared to unmodified GQDs [188]. At a concentration of 0.25 μg/mL, amino-N-GQDs exhibited a strong PDT effect which eliminated MDR species completely under 800 nm laser irritation for 12 s two-photon excitation. In another study, Chen et al. designed nanoparticles of novel MOFs (PCN-224(Zr/Ti)) which substituted the zirconium (Zr) clusters with titanium (Ti) in the frameworks of PCN-224 through simple and facile cation exchange [189]. While the Ti incorporation could greatly boost the generation of ROS, PCN-224(Zr/Ti) NPs were effective in eliminating both normal and MDR Gram-positive/negative pathogens under visible light. Moreover, it's certificated in vivo that the developed PCN-224(Zr/Ti)@PLGA dressing could prevent the inflammation and further accelerate the re-epithelization of MDR A. baumannii-induced and MDR E. coli-induced would healing without causing harm to the skin. Another strategy is multifunctional synergistic aPDT with different therapies. Besides the methods mentioned before [131,132,171,178,182,186], there are also other antimicrobial designs and strategies for future reference. In a study, Lu et al. constructed a multifunctional chitosan (CS) functionalized magnetic Chlorin e6 (CS-MP-Ce6) to combat MRSA [190]. To conclude, the CS-MP-Ce6 nanoparticles have three advanced properties: (i) magnetic polydopamine nanoparticles to enhance the enrichment performance of MRSA cells; (ii) to enhance the affinity of the therapeutic CS with the bacterial cells electronically; and (iii) when exposed to a magnetic field, the CS-MP-Ce6-bound MRSA cells immediately aggregate. In vitro, NIR-irritated CS-MP-Ce6 performed efficient capture of MRSA and good antibacterial ability under magnetic field – over 80 % of MRSA were inactivated. In another study, Xie et al. reported a simple and effective method for developing microparticles containing Ga3+ and ICG for eradicating MDR bacteria by synergizing PDT and iron metabolism blocking [191]. The photodynamic effect induced by ICG-Ga NPs could destroy the bacterial membrane, further enhance the endocytosis of Ga3+, then replace iron in bacterial cells to disrupt bacterial iron metabolism, and demonstrate the synergistic bacterial killing and biofilm disrupting effects. Integration of PTT/PDT in MDR therapy is also reported [192].

Thanks to versatile modifiability of nanomaterials, several combination therapies have already been applied to nanomaterials-based aPDT, such as bacteria targeted PDT and gas therapy. With precise bacteria targeting and excess gas cytotoxicity, nanomaterials-based aPDT combined with multiple therapies exhibits an outstanding antibacterial activity against MDR infection (Table 3 ).

Although nanomaterials-mediated aPDT has shown various advantages and achieved favorable therapeutic effects, there still exist some concerns that may limit the translation of nanomaterials-based aPDT into clinic application. Here, we summarize four concerns and provide possible solutions for each, including 1) the biosafety of nanomaterials in vivo, 2) the protein corona's effect on nanomaterials, 3) potential cytotoxicity induced by excessive ROS, and 4) the insufficient therapeutic penetration depth of PDT.

First, since the metabolism of nanomaterials in vivo is still uncertain, their biosafety needs to be reconsidered. It is well known that Ag, Au, Cu, and Zn nanoparticles may have toxic effects on human cells [193]. However, for 49 % of the Ag nanomaterial products currently in use, no further information on their composition has been provided [194]. A recent work has shown that the bioefficacy/toxicity of nanoparticles is mainly affected by the number of atoms/ions/molecules per nanoparticle, as well as their solubility and surface charge [195]. Therefore, developing nanomaterial-based delivery systems by engineering nanomaterials with specific size and surface may help avoid potential toxicity. More importantly, green nanotechnology, which synthesizes nanoparticles with the help of biochemicals from plants or microorganisms, is expected to prevent any associated nanotoxicity via implementation across the whole lifecycle of nanoformulation [196]. Recent studies have shown that green nanomaterials, such as green AgNPs and GO nanoribbons, possess safer and more environmental properties [197,198]. Second, the transport, biodistribution, biodegradation and biofunction of nanomaterials may be disturbed by the formation of protein corona in vivo [199,200]. Since the original surface ligand is covered by the protein corona, the final biological characteristics may be very different from the synthetic characteristics of the materials in the beginning, which may result in rapid clearance from the blood stream and loss of targeting capacity. To prolong the circulating time, one strategy is to modify nanomaterials with PEG or endogenous plasma proteins, such as albumin and ferritin [198]. For example, Yang et al. stabilized Au quantum clusters with the milk metalloprotein alpha-lactalbumin, which achieved longer circulation times as well as sustained tumor retention in vivo [201]. Third, although ROS possess a broad-spectrum antibacterial ability, additional ROS (especially free radicals) in aPDT holds a risk to induce iatrogenic inflammation [202], which calls for accurate target and controlled release in nanomaterials-based aPDT. Recently, ROS scavenging biomaterials have developed to limit inflammation and protect the host from damage [203]. The integration of ROS scavenging biomaterials such as endogenous antioxidant enzymes and nanozymes are a possible strategy to alleviate undesirable ROS in aPDT. For example, Sun et al. constructed a ceria-based nano-PS to clear the additional ROS in periodontal pockets after PDT, which simultaneously eliminated sterilization and inflammation via bidirectional regulation effect [204]. Finally, the limited penetration depth of light into tissue largely restricts the clinical applications of aPDT to only superficial lesions such as skin and oral bacterial infections, but hardly in systematic infections. This may be partially overcome by increasing the nano-PSs' sensitivity to light with chemical modification. Additionally, NIR-triggered and magnetic-directed nanotechnology can be another solution.

In summary, proper modifications of nanomaterials and combing nanomaterials-based aPDT with other therapies may provide promising strategies for improving their antibacterial efficacy and avoiding the potential risks. In the future, bio-friendly nanomaterials maybe a valuable direction in the field of nanomaterial-based aPDT.

Bacterial infection has been considered as a great health burden worldwide. Although antibiotics have been widely utilized as cost-effective agents in infection treatment, the extensively drug resistance greatly limits their antibacterial efficacy and may even lead to treatment failure. Therefore, it is significant to explore novel efficient antibacterial strategies. In our previous work, the functional nanomaterials, which serve as either direct photothermal agents (PAs) or advanced carriers for PAs, and its applications in antibacterial photothermal therapy was comprehensively reviewed [205]. In this present work, the applications of nanomaterials-based antibacterial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) are summarized. Recently, the development of aPDT that can damage biofilms and disrupt bacteria metabolism without triggering drug resistance has shown great potentials in treating bacterial infection, especially in MDR treatment. The basic mechanism of aPDT is to absorb the energy of light through PSs, producing ROS that can destroy the structure of bacterial cells, interfere with bacterial metabolism, and denature bacterial toxins and enzymes. With the characteristics of inherent antibacterial ability, favorable photocatalytic activity, surface plasmonic resonance, easy modification, and high drug loading efficacy, nanomaterials can serve as PSs or carriers of PSs to further improve the treatment effects of aPDT. Specifically, functional nanomaterials, such as AuNCs and CDots, not only have intrinsic antimicrobial activity, but also show more active photosensitivity and photocatalytic ability than traditional PSs, achieving better ROS productivity and deeper penetration. While non-functional nanomaterials, such as liposomes and polymer-based nanoparticles, can act as carriers for PSs to minimize the cytotoxicity, improve the hydrophilicity, and assist a sustained release. Furthermore, by combining with target therapy and gas therapy, nanomaterials-based aPDT could target specific bacteria, decreases damage to healthy cells, and improve the treatment efficacy to MDR bacteria. However, current research on nanomaterials-based aPDT is still in the laboratory stage and faces challenges in clinical translation, such as unknown metabolism of nanomaterials in vivo, uncontrollable drug release, and potential toxicity induced by excessive ROS. Considering the application of aPDT in clinical practice, nanomaterials as PSs should be designed with sufficient effective drug duration and biological functions to reduce inflammation and enhance tissue regeneration. Moreover, future research on how to modify antibacterial coatings on nanomaterials and properly integrate various strategies is also encouraged.

Ruijiao Yan: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Meijun Zhan: Writing – original draft. Jingchen Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qiang Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
